candidates.direction_name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To Single Stage
ranking_notes: This is the highest-information next move because it separates the 3-CTA thesis from the accidentally coupled two-stage export scratch change.
It stays on the accepted-base family and removes one clear source of extra shared memory before the loop spends another round on a broader pivot.
The historical repo already contains a close precedent for this exact export-scratch trim, so the implementation should stay bounded.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 7 was not a clean test of 3-CTA residency on the non-PTX sibling because the source still carried a two-stage export scratch lifetime from round 6. That inflated the sibling to 26,112 B shared memory per block while barrier rose to 11.31%. The cleanest next move is to keep the same grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling surface and the same 3-CTA residency target, but trim export scratch back to a single stage so the loop can see whether the barrier tax came from occupancy itself or from the coupled scratch growth.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Shared export lifetime and barrier tax on the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling, currently confounded with the 3-CTA residency probe.
candidates.risk: Low-moderate. This is a narrow rollback of one coupled shared/export choice on an otherwise already-correct surface.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory does not fall back toward the accepted-base class or if barrier remains in the 10%+ band after the scratch trim.
expected_local_changes: Keep the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling launch path and 3-CTA residency target.
Trim export scratch from the current two-stage lifetime back to the PTX-style single stage.

ranking_notes: Keep this same-surface retime alive because the current run still presents a textbook barrier-dominated failure mode after occupancy improved.
Rank it below dir_01 because the export-scratch confound is even cheaper to remove and makes the round-7 evidence cleaner.
candidates.hypothesis: If the export-scratch trim is not enough by itself, the next same-surface move is to retime the future-tile refill order on the non-PTX sibling. The current run already proved that registers and occupancy can move in the right direction; what remains is the barrier wall. Reordering the refill seam without changing tile shape or launch bounds is the cleanest next synchronization-only variant on this surface.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence at the seam between cp.async wait completion, __syncthreads(), and future-tile refill ordering on the non-PTX 3-CTA sibling.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is a small scheduler-seam edit, but it still depends on codegen and can easily move stalls without improving runtime.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if barrier does not improve materially or if runtime stays slower than the accepted base after the seam retime.
expected_local_changes: Keep the current grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling surface.
Retune the future-stage refill order around the barrier seam without changing launch bounds, tile shape, or export scratch depth.

candidates.direction_name: Reopen The 256x128 Half-Panel Register-Reuse Branch Later
ranking_notes: Keep this family alive because it still best matches the human 256x128 guidance.
Rank it third because the current accepted-base family still contains unresolved confounds that are cheaper to resolve first.
candidates.hypothesis: The 256x128 family should stay queued because it remains the closest match to the user's explicit tiling guidance and still has the largest theoretical register-budget upside. But the loop should not jump back there until the accepted-base family has been deconfounded. Right now the higher-value information comes from learning whether the non-PTX sibling can work once its export scratch and barrier seam are cleaned up.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register reuse, B-fragment lifetime, and writer-ownership constraints on the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot.
candidates.risk: High. This family remains broad and already consumed two clean-loop rounds without producing a viable surface.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop immediately on another 28-30 ms regression or any correctness failure; do not spend consecutive rounds here without a new machine-state change.
expected_local_changes: Keep the correctness-safe 256x128 writer ownership path queued.
Only reopen this family after the accepted-base 128x128 surface has been remeasured without the current export-scratch confound.
